questions.question: Which tobacco carcinogen is associated with pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Tobacco-specific nitrosamines, such as NNK, are potent carcinogens that induce mutations in the KRAS oncogene, common in pancreatic cancer.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism by which smoking promotes pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Tobacco carcinogens generate DNA adducts and mutations in genes such as KRAS and TP53, initiating pancreatic carcinogenesis.

questions.question: What approximate percentage of pancreatic cancers is attributed to smoking?
questions.explanation: Epidemiological studies indicate that between 20% and 30% of cases of pancreatic adenocarcinoma are attributable to tobacco consumption.

questions.question: How long after quitting smoking does the risk of pancreatic cancer reduce to baseline?
questions.explanation: The risk of pancreatic cancer in ex-smokers approaches that of non-smokers after 15-20 years of quitting tobacco.

questions.question: Which gene is most frequently mutated in tobacco-associated pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: The KRAS activating mutation is the most prevalent molecular alteration in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, favored by tobacco carcinogens.

questions.question: What histological type of pancreatic cancer is most related to smoking?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma represents more than 90% of cases and is the subtype with the strongest association with smoking.

questions.question: What is the most common oncogenic mutation in pancreatic adenocarcinoma?
questions.explanation: The KRAS mutation is the most common genetic alteration in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, present in more than 90% of cases, constitutively activating the MAPK pathway.

questions.question: Which signaling pathway is constitutively activated by KRAS mutation in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Mutated KRAS permanently activates the MAPK-ERK pathway, promoting uncontrolled cell proliferation; It is the main pathway associated with this oncoprotein in pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

questions.question: What type of point mutation in KRAS is typically associated with pancreatic adenocarcinoma?
questions.explanation: The most common KRAS mutation in pancreatic cancer occurs at codon 12 (especially G12D), which prevents GTP hydrolysis and keeps the protein constitutively active.

questions.question: What is the main functional effect of KRAS mutation in pancreatic adenocarcinoma cells?
questions.explanation: Mutated KRAS promotes cell proliferation and survival, inhibiting apoptosis; this contributes to resistance to programmed cell death and aggressive tumor growth.

questions.question: ¿Qué fármaco inhibidor de KRAS G12C ha mostrado eficacia en adenocarcinoma pancreático con esa mutación específica?
questions.explanation: Sotorasib is a selective KRAS G12C inhibitor approved for lung cancer, but it has also been studied in pancreatic adenocarcinoma with this mutation, although the frequency of G12C is low in pancreas.

questions.question: What histologic feature is frequently associated with KRAS mutation in pancreatic adenocarcinoma?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma usually presents gland formation (ductal differentiation) associated with KRAS mutation, which is an early event in pancreatic carcinogenesis.

questions.question: What type of molecule is CA 19-9?
questions.explanation: CA 19-9 is a carbohydrate antigen (sialyl-Lewis a) that is expressed on the surface of pancreatic tumor cells and released into the bloodstream.

questions.question: What is the main clinical utility of CA 19-9 in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: CA 19-9 is mainly used to monitor the evolution and response to treatment in patients with already diagnosed pancreatic adenocarcinoma, not for population screening.

questions.question: What benign condition can falsely elevate CA 19-9?
questions.explanation: Chronic pancreatitis and other benign pancreatic pathologies can elevate CA 19-9, which limits its specificity and makes differential diagnosis difficult.

questions.question: In which blood group can CA 19-9 be undetectable?
questions.explanation: CA 19-9 is the sialyl-Lewis a antigen; Individuals with the Lewis a-b- phenotype do not express this antigen, so they may have undetectable levels.

questions.question: What is the serum CA 19-9 value most suggestive of pancreatic malignancy?
questions.explanation: Very high levels of CA 19-9 (>1000 U/mL) are strongly associated with advanced pancreatic adenocarcinoma, although they are not pathognomonic.

questions.question: What factor limits the sensitivity of CA 19-9 in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Small or early-stage tumors may not secrete enough CA 19-9, which reduces the sensitivity of the marker for early detection.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of obstructive jaundice in the head of the pancreas?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma is the most common malignant neoplasm in the pancreatic head, compressing the distal common bile duct and producing obstructive jaundice.

questions.question: What classic clinical sign accompanies obstructive jaundice due to pancreatic head tumor?
questions.explanation: The Courvoisier sign (palpable and non-painful gallbladder) is characteristic of obstructive jaundice due to neoplasia of the head of the pancreas, distinguishing it from stone causes.

questions.question: What imaging study is the initial choice to suspect obstructive jaundice due to pancreatic head tumor?
questions.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the initial study due to its availability, low cost and ability to detect bile duct dilation and mass in the head of the pancreas.

questions.question: What type of bilirubin predominates in obstructive jaundice due to pancreatic head cancer?
questions.explanation: In extrahepatic biliary obstruction, direct (conjugated) bilirubin predominates because the liver conjugates bilirubin but cannot excrete it into the intestine.

questions.question: What tumor marker is frequently elevated in adenocarcinoma of the head of the pancreas?
questions.explanation: CA 19-9 is the most used serum marker in pancreatic cancer; although it is not specific, it is elevated in most cases of ductal adenocarcinoma.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical treatment for resectable pancreatic head cancer?
questions.explanation: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) is the standard curative surgery for resectable tumors of the head of the pancreas, removing the head, duodenum, and distal bile duct.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of jaundice in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Jaundice in pancreatic cancer is mainly due to extrinsic compression of the common bile duct by the tumor in the pancreatic head, obstructing bile flow.

questions.question: What type of pain is characteristic of pancreatic cancer?
options: Dull pain in epigastrium with dorsal irradiation
questions.explanation: The pain in pancreatic cancer is usually dull, deep, located in the epigastrium and radiates to the back, worsening when lying down and improving when leaning forward.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding is frequently associated with weight loss in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: De novo hyperglycemia or new-onset diabetes is common in pancreatic cancer due to the destruction of pancreatic islets and insulin resistance, contributing to weight loss.

questions.question: What clinical sign is characteristic of obstructive jaundice due to pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Obstructive jaundice produces acholia (light stools) and choluria (dark urine) due to lack of bilirubin in the intestine and renal excretion of conjugated bilirubin respectively.

questions.question: What is the recommended initial imaging method when pancreatic cancer is suspected?
questions.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is usually the first study due to its availability and absence of radiation, although helical computed tomography with contrast is the test of choice for diagnosis and staging.

questions.question: What factor directly contributes to weight loss in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency due to tumor destruction reduces the production of digestive enzymes, causing poor absorption of fats and proteins, which contributes significantly to weight loss.

questions.question: What characterizes the Courvoisier-Terrier sign?
questions.explanation: The Courvoisier-Terrier sign describes a palpable, non-painful gallbladder associated with obstructive jaundice due to pancreatic neoplasia, unlike acute cholecystitis.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of Courvoisier-Terrier sign?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic head cancer is the most common cause of Courvoisier-Terrier sign, as it obstructs the distal bile duct and distends the gallbladder without inflammation.

questions.question: Which sonographic finding supports the Courvoisier-Terrier sign?
questions.explanation: In the Courvoisier-Terrier sign, the gallbladder appears distended and without parietal thickening, reflecting chronic distal obstruction without an acute inflammatory process.

questions.question: What clinical sign does Courvoisier-Terrier rule out?
options: Fever and pain in the right upper quadrant
questions.explanation: The presence of fever and pain in the right upper quadrant suggests acute cholecystitis or cholangitis, not the Courvoisier-Terrier sign, which occurs without pain or inflammatory signs.

questions.question: What is the prognostic implication of the Courvoisier-Terrier sign?
options: It is associated with resolvable choledocholithiasis
questions.explanation: The Courvoisier-Terrier sign usually indicates an advanced pancreatic neoplasia, frequently unresectable, with a poor short-term prognosis.

questions.question: What test confirms the cause of Courvoisier-Terrier sign?
questions.explanation: Contrast-enhanced computed tomography is the test of choice to identify and stage the pancreatic tumor causing Courvoisier-Terrier sign.

questions.question: What is the anatomical location of Klatskin tumor?
questions.explanation: Klatskin tumor is a cholangiocarcinoma that originates at the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts at the level of the hepatic hilum.

questions.question: What is the main risk factor for developing Klatskin tumor?
questions.explanation: Primary sclerosing cholangitis is the most important risk factor for hilar cholangiocarcinoma, with a cumulative incidence of up to 20% in these patients.

questions.question: What is the most common presenting symptom of Klatskin tumor?
questions.explanation: Painless obstructive jaundice is the most common clinical manifestation, due to obstruction of the upper bile confluence by the hilar tumor.

questions.question: What imaging study is the gold standard for staging Klatskin tumor?
questions.explanation: Magnetic resonance cholangiography (MRCP) is the technique of choice to evaluate ductal extension and resectability of Klatskin tumor.

questions.question: What is the most used surgical classification for Klatskin tumor?
questions.explanation: The Bismuth-Corlette classification stratifies Klatskin tumor according to the level of biliary obstruction in the hepatic ducts, guiding the surgical approach.

questions.question: What is the curative treatment of choice for Klatskin tumor?
questions.explanation: Surgical resection with negative margins (R0) is the only curative option; includes hepatectomy with bile duct resection and regional lymphadenectomy.

questions.question: What is the main usefulness of helical CT in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Helical CT is the method of choice for locoregional staging of pancreatic cancer, allowing vascular invasion to be evaluated and whether the tumor is resectable.

questions.question: Which phase of spiral CT is most useful to evaluate pancreatic tumor?
questions.explanation: The late arterial phase (or pancreatic phase) allows the best enhancement of the pancreatic parenchyma and the tumor, facilitating the detection and delimitation of the neoplasia.

questions.question: What criterion on helical CT suggests unresectability due to vascular invasion?
questions.explanation: Stenosis or occlusion of the portal or superior mesenteric vein by the tumor is a criterion of unresectability, since it indicates vascular compromise that prevents a complete resection.

questions.question: What is the approximate sensitivity of helical CT to detect liver metastases in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Multiphase helical CT has a sensitivity of 70-80% for detecting small liver metastases, although MRI may be superior for lesions smaller than 1 cm.

questions.question: Which finding on helical CT is characteristic of pancreatic adenocarcinoma?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma is typically hypovascular in the arterial phase and presents late enhancement due to its dense desmoplastic stroma, which helps differentiate it from other lesions.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is primarily evaluated to decide resectability on helical CT?
questions.explanation: The superior mesenteric artery and the celiac trunk are the key arterial structures; its tumor involvement (>180° contact) defines unresectability in CT staging.

questions.question: What is the main indication for Whipple surgery?
questions.explanation: Whipple surgery is indicated primarily for resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma, as it is the only curative option for this malignancy.

questions.question: What structures are resected in a classic pancreatoduodenectomy?
questions.explanation: The classic pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) resectes the pancreatic head, duodenum, gallbladder and distal common bile duct, partially preserving the spleen and stomach.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication after Whipple surgery?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic fistula is the most serious and frequent complication after a pancreatoduodenectomy, with high morbidity and mortality if it is not managed properly.

questions.question: What criterion contraindicates Whipple surgery in adenocarcinoma?
questions.explanation: Liver metastases contraindicate curative resection as they indicate disseminated disease; Whipple surgery is only curative in localized disease.

questions.question: What type of reconstruction is performed after a pancreatoduodenectomy?
questions.explanation: Typical reconstruction includes pancreatojejunostomy, choledochojejunostomy, and gastrojejunostomy, restoring digestive transit after resection.

questions.question: What preoperative study is essential to evaluate resectability?
questions.explanation: Triphasic (pancreatic) computed tomography allows evaluating local extension, vascular invasion and distant metastasis, being the standard for deciding resectability.

questions.question: What is the first-line chemotherapy regimen in metastatic pancreatic cancer with good performance status?
questions.explanation: FOLFIRINOX is the first-line regimen in patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer and good performance status (ECOG 0-1), demonstrating superiority in overall survival compared to gemcitabine.

questions.question: What drug combination does the FOLFIRINOX regimen include?
questions.explanation: FOLFIRINOX combines oxaliplatin, irinotecan and 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) in continuous infusion, plus leucovorin; This combination has shown greater efficacy than gemcitabine in selected patients.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of gemcitabine over FOLFIRINOX in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Gemcitabine has a more favorable toxicity profile, with a lower incidence of febrile neutropenia and severe diarrhea, being an option for patients with worse functional status or significant comorbidities.

questions.question: At what stage of pancreatic cancer is FOLFIRINOX considered as a neoadjuvant option?
questions.explanation: FOLFIRINOX is used as neoadjuvant chemotherapy in locally advanced pancreatic cancer (non-metastatic but not initially resectable) to try to shrink the tumor and allow subsequent surgery.

questions.question: What is the standard adjuvant chemotherapy regimen in resected pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: The PRODIGE 24 study demonstrated that modified FOLFIRINOX (without 5-FU bolus) as adjuvant treatment prolongs disease-free and overall survival compared to gemcitabine, which is the current standard.

questions.question: Which serious adverse effect is most characteristic of irinotecan in the FOLFIRINOX regimen?
questions.explanation: Irinotecan produces late diarrhea (due to topoisomerase I inhibition) that can be severe and requires management with loperamide and monitoring; It is a distinctive adverse effect of this drug.

questions.question: What is the most common organ for pancreatic cancer to metastasize?
questions.explanation: The liver is the most common site of distant metastasis of pancreatic cancer due to portal venous drainage that facilitates hematogenous spread from the pancreas.

questions.question: What route of spread explains the high frequency of liver metastases in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: The portal vein drains blood from the pancreas directly to the liver, allowing pancreatic tumor cells to easily reach the liver parenchyma and metastasize.

questions.question: What is the most common clinical presentation of liver metastases in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Obstructive jaundice is common in liver metastases from pancreatic cancer, since the lesions can compress the intrahepatic or extrahepatic bile duct, obstructing bile flow.

questions.question: What imaging finding is characteristic of liver metastases from pancreatic cancer?
options: Hypovascular nodules in the portal phase
Hyperuptake lesions in the arterial phase
questions.explanation: Liver metastases from pancreatic adenocarcinoma are usually hypovascular, so in the portal phase of CT scan they appear as hypodense nodules compared to normal liver parenchyma.

questions.question: Which tumor marker is frequently elevated in patients with hepatic metastatic pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: CA 19-9 is the most used tumor marker in pancreatic cancer, and its serum levels are usually elevated in the presence of liver metastases, although it is not specific for the diagnosis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in pancreatic cancer with liver metastases?
questions.explanation: In the presence of liver metastases, pancreatic cancer is considered unresectable, so the standard treatment is palliative chemotherapy, usually with regimens based on gemcitabine or FOLFIRINOX.

questions.question: What is the 5-year overall survival for pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic cancer has a very unfavorable prognosis, with a 5-year survival rate of less than 10%, due to its late diagnosis and biological aggressiveness.

questions.question: What factor contributes most to the poor prognosis of pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Late diagnosis, when the disease is already metastatic or locally advanced, is the main factor in poor prognosis, limiting curative options.

questions.question: What is the median survival in metastatic pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: In metastatic disease, the median survival is 3 to 6 months without treatment, and with chemotherapy it can be extended to around 6 to 11 months.

questions.question: What percentage of patients with pancreatic cancer are candidates for curative surgery at diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Only about 15-20% of patients have localized, resectable disease at the time of diagnosis, limiting curative options.

questions.question: What is the main cause of low survival in pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: Late clinical presentation, with nonspecific symptoms until advanced stages, prevents early detection and drastically reduces survival.

questions.question: What adverse prognostic factor is associated with pancreatic cancer?
questions.explanation: The KRAS oncogene mutation is present in more than 90% of pancreatic adenocarcinomas and is associated with a worse prognosis and resistance to therapies.
